name: Band Lat Pulldown
resistance_profile: Band anchored overhead; tension rises as it stretches, so the pulldown is hardest at the fully contracted bottom position — a shortened-biased vertical pull.
cues: Chest tall, pull the elbows toward the floor
Lead with the elbows, not the hands
Reach into a full overhead stretch each rep
execution_steps: Anchor a resistance band to a secure point overhead such as a pull-up bar, door anchor, or rack.
Kneel or sit below the anchor and grip the band with both hands, arms extended overhead under light tension.
Set your chest tall and pull the band down toward your upper chest, driving the elbows down and back.
Squeeze the shoulder blades down as the hands reach chest or shoulder level.
Control the band back to a full overhead stretch and repeat.
good_when: Training at home or traveling with only a band.
You want to learn the lat-pulldown pattern and feel the lats before loading a machine.
You want a joint-friendly warm-up for vertical pulling.
bad_when: You need heavy progressive overload beyond band tension.
You have no sturdy overhead anchor point.
common_errors: Leaning back and turning it into a row
Bending the elbows early so the biceps take over
Letting the band snap back instead of controlling the return
progressions: Use a thicker band or step further from the anchor for more tension.
Progress to a weighted lat pulldown or assisted pull-up.
regressions: Use a lighter band or pull one arm at a time.
Perform seated with a reduced range if the full stretch is hard to control.
